examples.title: FIFO Page Replacement Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of First In First Out page replacement
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FIFO_Page_Replacement
INPUT: Reference String R[], Frames F, Number of References N
OUTPUT: Number of Page Faults

BEGIN
  SET pageFaults = 0
  SET queue = empty queue
  SET frames[F] = -1 initially (empty)
  
  FOR i = 0 to N-1 DO
    currentPage = R[i]
    
    IF currentPage NOT in frames THEN
      pageFaults = pageFaults + 1
      
      IF queue size < F THEN
        // Add new page to queue
        ENQUEUE(queue, currentPage)
        frames[currentPage] = currentPage
      ELSE
        // Replace oldest page (front of queue)
        oldPage = DEQUEUE(queue)
        frames[oldPage] = -1
        ENQUEUE(queue, currentPage)
        frames[currentPage] = currentPage
      END IF
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  RETURN pageFaults
END
examples.explanation: FIFO maintains pages in arrival order using a queue. When replacement is needed, the page at the front (oldest) is removed and new page added at the back.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(f) where f is number of frames

examples.title: LRU Page Replacement Implementation
examples.description: Least Recently Used page replacement using counters
examples.code:
def LRU_Page_Replacement(reference_string, num_frames):
    # Initialize frames with -1 (empty)
    frames = [-1] * num_frames
    # Counter for each frame to track usage
    counters = [0] * num_frames
    page_faults = 0
    time = 1
    
    for page in reference_string:
        # Check if page is already in memory
        if page not in frames:
            page_faults += 1
            # Find LRU frame (minimum counter)
            if -1 in frames:
                # Find first empty frame
                empty_idx = frames.index(-1)
                frames[empty_idx] = page
                counters[empty_idx] = time
            else:
                # Find least recently used
                lru_idx = counters.index(min(counters))
                frames[lru_idx] = page
                counters[lru_idx] = time
        else:
            # Update counter for accessed page
            idx = frames.index(page)
            counters[idx] = time
        
        time += 1
        print(f"Page {page}: Frames {frames}, Faults: {page_faults}")
    
    return page_faults

# Example usage
ref_string = [1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5]
print(f"Total Page Faults: {LRU_Page_Replacement(ref_string, 3)}")
examples.explanation: LRU implementation uses counters to track page access time. When page fault occurs, the frame with minimum counter (least recently used) is replaced.

examples.title: Second Chance (Clock) Algorithm
examples.description: Clock algorithm using reference bits for page replacement
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Second_Chance_Clock
INPUT: Reference String R[], Frames F
OUTPUT: Number of Page Faults

BEGIN
  SET pageFaults = 0
  SET frames[F] = initially empty (-1)
  SET refBits[F] = initially 0
  SET clockHand = 0 (points to first frame)
  
  FOR each page in reference string DO
    IF page NOT in frames THEN
      pageFaults = pageFaults + 1
      
      // Find victim page using clock algorithm
      WHILE TRUE DO
        IF frames[clockHand] == -1 THEN
          // Empty frame found
          BREAK
        ELSE IF refBits[clockHand] == 0 THEN
          // No recent use, replace this page
          BREAK
        ELSE
          // Page used recently, give second chance
          refBits[clockHand] = 0
          clockHand = (clockHand + 1) mod F
        END IF
      END WHILE
      
      // Replace victim with new page
      frames[clockHand] = page
      refBits[clockHand] = 1
      clockHand = (clockHand + 1) mod F
    ELSE
      // Page hit - set reference bit
      index = find index of page in frames
      refBits[index] = 1
    END IF
  END FOR
  
  RETURN pageFaults
END
examples.explanation: Clock algorithm scans circular buffer. If page's reference bit is 1, clear it and move on. If 0, replace that page. This approximates LRU with lower overhead.

examples.title: Optimal Page Replacement
examples.description: Simulates Optimal algorithm for comparison (requires future knowledge)
examples.explanation: Optimal algorithm looks ahead in reference string to find page that won't be used for the longest time. This gives minimum possible page faults and serves as benchmark.
